code: # 1. Fit fit <- fit_response(   data    = my_data,   spend   = "ad_spend",   kpi     = "conversions",   date    = "week",   units   = "impressions",   # optional   type    = "gompertz",   midpoint_range = c(0.1, 0.5),   ceiling_max    = 3 )  # 2. Inspect print(fit) plot(fit)            # dashboard: response + AR/MR + cost-per plot(fit, type = "diagnostics")  # trace plots + PPCs  # 3. Analyze mrm_params(fit) mrm_summary(fit)  # 4. Compare models mrm_plot_compare(list(gompertz = fit_g, logistic = fit_l), layout = "overlay")  # 5. Optimize (point estimate — fast, single solution) opt <- opt_mix(list(ch1 = fit1, ch2 = fit2), budget = 500000) print(opt) plot(opt) summary(opt)  # 5b. Optimize (posterior — distribution of solutions) opt_post <- opt_mix(list(ch1 = fit1, ch2 = fit2),                     method = "posterior", budget = 500000, n_draws = 200) plot(opt_post, type = "posterior")  # 5c. Period budgets (e.g., $10M annual) opt_annual <- opt_mix(models, budget = 10000000, n_weeks = 52)  # 6. Visualize on response curves plot(opt, type = "curves")     # response curves with current + optimal points plot(opt, type = "returns")    # AR/MR curves with current + optimal points  # 7. Compare two optimization results comp <- compare(opt, opt_post) plot(comp, type = "spend")     # dumbbell chart summary(opt)                   # tidy comparison tibble with deltas

title: mrmopt Package — Project Memory
text: Single function two methods: - method = "point" (default): Uses posterior median parameters → single nloptr solve (~1s) - method = "posterior": Optimizes N posterior draws → distribution solutions (~6s 200 draws × 9 channels)

title: mrmopt Package — Project Memory
text: posterior path uses raw parameter draws + rm_dispatch() instead brms::posterior_epred() — 10,000x speedup makes posterior optimization practical.
code: opt_mix() ├─ hlpr_auto_constraints() / hlpr_parse_constraints()  # constraint setup ├─ opt_mix_point()          # point-estimate path │  ├─ mrm_response_function()  # extract median curve │  └─ hlpr_opt_solve()         # nloptr COBYLA └─ opt_mix_posterior()      # posterior path    ├─ hlpr_extract_draws()     # pre-extract & unscale all draws (fast path)    ├─ make_draw_objective()    # build objective from raw draws + rm_dispatch    └─ hlpr_opt_solve() × N    # one solve per draw (optionally parallel)

title: Model Diagnostics and Curve Comparison
text: trace plot shows sampled parameter value every post-warmup iteration, coloured chain. well-converged model shows: Good mixing: chains interleave freely one another — chain stays consistently others. Stationarity: traces fluctuate around stable central value without drifting upward downward iterations. Similar variance: chains explore roughly range. Warning signs look : Stuck chains: one chain sits different value others extended stretches. Funnel geometry: one chain much lower variance others, suggesting trapped narrow region posterior. Drift trend: slow upward downward movement suggests chain reached stationarity. appear, try increasing adapt_delta (e.g., control = list(adapt_delta = 0.99)), tightening priors, increasing number warmup iterations.

title: Model Diagnostics and Curve Comparison
text: trace plot gives visual impression; two numerical summaries give formal verdict. R-hat (potential scale reduction factor) measures convergence comparing within-chain variance -chain variance. chains mixed well, equal R-hat ≈ 1. Values 1.01 warning sign; 1.05 indicate convergence problem. ESS (effective sample size) measures many independent samples chains worth, accounting autocorrelation. Low ESS means posterior estimate based fewer independent draws nominal sample count. rough rule thumb, aim ESS > 400 reliable estimates. extracted brms summary: Healthy values Rhat ≤ 1.01 ESS well 400 bulk tail. Tail ESS particularly important credible interval accuracy — 2.5th 97.5th percentiles estimated fewer effective draws mean. inspect full brms convergence output:

title: Fitting and Analyzing Response Curve Models
text: fit_response() entry point model fitting. minimum needs data frame, names spend KPI columns, date column, curve type. note arguments: type: response curve family. See theory vignette guidance choosing six options. auto = TRUE (default): mrmopt scales data [0, 1] fitting sets sensible default priors automatically. removes need specify priors hand channels. anchor_zero = TRUE (default): injects synthetic (spend = 0, conversions = 0) data point. encodes domain assumption zero spend produces zero response anchors floor curve. Disable believe channel generates baseline conversions independent paid spend. chains, iter, warmup: passed directly Stan. values reduced illustration speed. production fits, use defaults (chains = 4, iter = 4000, warmup = 1000). units: optional column media units (impressions, clicks, GRPs). supplied, cost-per-unit metrics added throughout output. Covered units section .
code: fit <- fit_response(   data   = paid_search,   spend  = "spend",   kpi    = "conversions",   date   = "week",   type   = "gompertz",   chains = 2,   iter   = 1000,   warmup = 500,   seed   = 8214 )

title: Fitting and Analyzing Response Curve Models
text: Calling print(fit) — just typing fit console — displays structured summary fitted model. output five sections: Header — channel name (taken spend column), curve type, number weeks training data, whether zero anchor used. Current Performance — metrics evaluated observed average weekly spend. tells channel operating right now: Weekly Spend KPI: average week. AR (Absolute Return): conversions per dollar total spend — .e., average efficiency whole budget. MR (Marginal Return): conversions generated next dollar — .e., efficiency incremental spend current level. CP (Cost per conversion): dollars spent per conversion. Parameters — four fitted curve parameters original data units. parameter shown posterior mean 95% credible interval. Response Curve Summary — three analytically important points curve, reported spend, KPI, AR, MR, CP: Range Min: spend peak marginal return — efficient point begin investing. level, additional dollar efficient last. Range Peak: spend peak absolute return — single efficient total spend level. Range Max: spend marginal return decayed 70% peak — point diminishing returns accelerate meaningfully. %Wks column shows fraction observed weeks fell , within, range. Bayes R² — posterior distribution proportion variance KPI explained model. Higher better; credible interval reflects uncertainty fit quality. underlying data summary also accessible programmatically:

title: Fitting and Analyzing Response Curve Models
text: focused view four curve parameters plain-language descriptions, use mrm_params_summary(): Interpreting context Paid Search: Ceiling (d): theoretical maximum weekly conversions channel achieve unlimited spend. posterior uncertainty around parameter often wide — data rarely extend fully saturated region. Midpoint (e): spend level curve’s inflection point. Gompertz curves curve sits ~37% range, peak efficiency zone begins somewhat e. Steepness (b): controls quickly curve transitions low high response. negative values mean sharper transition. Floor (c): baseline conversion level approached zero spend. anchor_zero = TRUE typically close zero. programmatic access raw posterior estimates (centre, lower, upper credible bounds named lists), use mrm_params():

text: plot(fit) produces three-panel dashboard combining three core views model. three-panel dashboard: response curve (top left), AR/MR returns (top right), cost per conversion (bottom). Response curve (top left): fitted curve 95% credible band, overlaid observed spend-conversion data points. vertical dashed lines mark three range points summary. current average weekly spend indicated solid vertical line. Returns (top right): absolute return (AR, conversions per dollar total spend) marginal return (MR, conversions per next dollar) plotted spend. gap widens spend increases diminishing returns set . Cost per conversion (bottom): inverse AR — dollars required per conversion — credible ribbon reflecting posterior uncertainty. Cost per conversion rises steeply spend pushes saturated region curve. code: set.seed(3071) paid_search_with_units <- paid_search |>   mutate(     impressions = as.integer(round(spend / 12 * 1000 + rnorm(n(), 0, 500)))   ) fit_units <- fit_response(   data   = paid_search_with_units,   spend  = "spend",   kpi    = "conversions",   date   = "week",   units  = "impressions",   type   = "gompertz",   chains = 2,   iter   = 1000,   warmup = 500,   seed   = 4417 ) plot(fit_units, x_var = "units") # Cost per unit stored on the model fit_units$cost_per_unit #> [1] 0.01199984 fit_units #> -- Response Curve Summary: gompertz ------------------------------------------  #> Channel: spend #> Weeks: 104 | Anchor: yes #> -- Current Performance -------------------------------------------------------  #> Weekly Spend: $44,963  |  Weekly Units: 3,746,940 #> KPI: 582  |  CP: $78  |  AR: 0.0114  |  MR: 0.0561 #> -- Parameters ----------------------------------------------------------------  #>   b (growth rate):     -2.02e-04 #>   c (floor):           70 #>   d (ceiling):         949 #>   e (midpoint):        $41,918 #> -- Response Curve Summary ----------------------------------------------------  #>   Min (peak MR):     $41,967  ->  KPI: 397  |  CP: $106 #>   Peak (peak AR):    $53,760  ->  KPI: 872  |  CP: $62 #>   Max (70% MR):      $46,939  ->  KPI: 681  |  CP: $69 #>  #> 33.7% of weeks below range | 23.1% in range | 43.3% above range #> -- Bayes R2 ------------------------------------------------------------------  #>   R2: 0.9926 (95% CI: [0.9919, 0.9928]) #>  #> Use summary(x) for brms model diagnostics.

title: Fitting and Analyzing Response Curve Models
text: mrm_prior() provides scale-invariant controls three intuitive quantities: midpoint_range: curve’s inflection point can fall, expressed fraction observed spend range. example, c(0.2, 0.7) says inflection point somewhere 20% 70% way minimum maximum observed spend. ceiling_max: ceiling parameter can multiple observed maximum KPI. value 2 says don’t expect channel ever deliver double historical best week. floor_min: lower asymptote least value original KPI units.
code: my_prior <- mrm_prior(   midpoint_range = c(0.2, 0.7),   ceiling_max    = 2,   floor_min      = 0 )  fit_simplified <- fit_response(   data  = paid_search, spend = "spend", kpi = "conversions",   date  = "week", type = "gompertz",   prior = my_prior )

title: Media Mix Optimization
text: fitted response curves media channel, opt_mix() finds budget allocation maximises total KPI. supports two optimization methods: Point estimate (method = "point"): uses posterior median parameters model. Fast — typically one second. Posterior sampling (method = "posterior"): optimises many posterior draws, producing distribution optimal allocations reflects Bayesian uncertainty. Takes seconds. vignette walks full optimization workflow using mrmopt_data example dataset.

title: Media Mix Optimization
text: start fitting response curve channel. practice compare multiple curve types per channel select best fit (see Model Comparison & Diagnostics vignette). use Gompertz curves simplicity. result named list mrmfit objects — required input opt_mix().
code: data(mrmopt_data)  channels <- c("Paid Search", "Paid Social", "Display",               "Online Video", "TV")  models <- list() for (ch in channels) {   d <- mrmopt_data |> filter(channel == ch)      # Use log_logistic for TV (log-scale channel), gompertz for the rest   curve_type <- if (ch == "TV") "log_logistic" else "gompertz"      models[[ch]] <- fit_response(     data = d, spend = "spend", kpi = "conversions", date = "week",     type = curve_type   ) }

title: Response Curve Theory and Data Requirements
text: Media channels deliver returns proportion spend. first dollar spent channel typically efficient — reaches responsive audience, captures highest-intent search queries, occupies cheapest inventory. spend increases, additional dollar reaches progressively less responsive audiences, drives incrementally smaller lifts, encounters diminishing returns. behaviour well-described S-shaped saturation curve: response rises steeply first, passes inflection point, gradually levels toward asymptotic ceiling. shape encodes two important economic realities: Diminishing marginal returns — incremental KPI generated additional dollar spend falls total spend grows. biological capacity ceiling — many consumers market, much demand capture. Linear models represent either property. response curve model fits nonlinear, parametric relationship spend KPI captures .

what: A Common Mathematical Language: The Four-Parameter Family
title: Response Curve Theory and Data Requirements
text: six response curves mrmopt share four parameters, giving unified interpretation regardless curve type: range curve — total vertical travel — d−cd - c. floor c rarely exactly zero practice; can absorb baseline effects organic response occurs even without paid media. steepness parameter b governs transition speed floor ceiling. negative b produces near step-function: nearly response achieved within narrow spend band. b closer zero produces slow, gradual rise.

title: Response Curve Theory and Data Requirements
text: Three spend levels partition curve interpretable zones: Range min (peak MR): spend level dollar efficient. Spending level means leaving efficiency table. Range peak (peak AR): spend level total return per dollar (absolute return) maximised — single efficient operating point. Range max (MR decay threshold): spend level marginal return declined specified fraction peak (default 70%). Beyond , diminishing returns accelerate meaningfully. Gompertz curve annotated three operating range points.
code:
x_range <- seq(0, 120000, length.out = 1000) y_gompertz <- mrmopt:::rm_Gompertz(x_range, b = -0.00012, c = 50, d = 900, e = 50000) mr_g <- c(NA, diff(y_gompertz))  peak_mr_x  <- x_range[which.max(mr_g)] peak_ar_x  <- x_range[which.max(y_gompertz / x_range)] decay_mr   <- 0.7 * max(mr_g, na.rm = TRUE) decay_x    <- x_range[which(mr_g <= decay_mr & x_range > peak_mr_x)[1]]  tibble(x = x_range, y = y_gompertz) |>   ggplot(aes(x, y)) +   geom_line(linewidth = 1, colour = "#2d6fa6") +   geom_vline(xintercept = peak_mr_x, linetype = "dashed", colour = "#e07b39") +   geom_vline(xintercept = peak_ar_x, linetype = "dashed", colour = "#3aa36e") +   geom_vline(xintercept = decay_x,   linetype = "dashed", colour = "#c94040") +   annotate("text", x = peak_mr_x, y = 150, label = "Peak MR
(range min)",            hjust = -0.1, size = 3, colour = "#e07b39") +   annotate("text", x = peak_ar_x, y = 250, label = "Peak AR
(range peak)",            hjust = -0.1, size = 3, colour = "#3aa36e") +   annotate("text", x = decay_x,   y = 350, label = "70% MR decay

title: Response Curve Theory and Data Requirements
text: six response curves mrmopt share unified four-parameter structure. key distinctions : Symmetry: Logistic Log-Logistic symmetric around inflection point; Gompertz family asymmetric. Scale: Linear-scale curves (Logistic, Gompertz, Reflected Gompertz) interior marginal return peak; log-scale curves (Log-Logistic, Weibull, Reflected Weibull) monotonically decreasing marginal returns. Inflection timing: Within scale group, standard, Gompertz, reflected Gompertz variants place inflection 50%, ~37%, ~63% response range respectively. Fitting begins fit_response(). next vignette covers full fitting analysis workflow using realistic simulated dataset.

title: Define a response form for a nonlinear model — hlpr_define_response_form
text: function supports following response forms: logistic: \(y = c + ((d - c)/(1 + exp(b*(x - e))))\) log_logistic: \(y = c + ((d - c)/(1 + exp(b*(log(x) - log(e))))\) gompertz: \(y = c + (d - c) * exp(-exp(b * (x - e)))\) reflected_gompertz: \(y = c + (d - c) * (1 - exp(-exp(b * (-x + e))))\) weibull: \(y = c + (d - c) * exp(-exp(b * (log(x) - log(e))))\) reflected_weibull: \(y = c + (d - c) * (1 - exp(-exp(b * (-log(x) + log(e))))\)

title: Create a prior specification for response curve fitting — mrm_prior

code: mrm_prior(midpoint_range = c(0.1, 0.9), ceiling_max = 5, floor_min = 0)

title: Create a prior specification for response curve fitting — mrm_prior
text: midpoint_range two-element numeric vector specifying lower upper bounds midpoint (inflection point) fractions x-axis range. Values 0 1. example, `c(0.1, 0.9)` means midpoint expected fall 10th 90th percentile x range. Default `c(0.1, 0.9)`. ceiling_max multiplier observed maximum response variable, defining upper bound upper asymptote (ceiling). example, `ceiling_max = 3` means ceiling can 3 times observed max y. Must >= 1. Default `5`. floor_min scalar specifying lower bound lower asymptote (floor), original data units. Default `0`, meaning response go zero.

title: Create a prior specification for response curve fitting — mrm_prior
text: object class `mrm_prior` containing prior specification.

title: Create a prior specification for response curve fitting — mrm_prior
text: response curve model four parameters: floor (c) Lower asymptote — minimum response value. ceiling (d) Upper asymptote — maximum response value. steepness (b) Growth rate controlling sharply curve rises. midpoint (e) Inflection point — curve reaches half range. function allows set constraints midpoint ceiling, parameters users typically intuition . Steepness managed internally broad, scale-aware defaults. floor defaults zero can overridden models non-zero baselines. full control, can bypass interface pass raw prior object directly fit_response.

title: Create a prior specification for response curve fitting — mrm_prior
code: # Default priors mrm_prior() #> mrm_prior specification: #>   midpoint range : [0.1, 0.9] (x-axis fraction) #>   ceiling max    : 5 x observed max of y #>   floor min      : 0 (original data units)  # Expect midpoint in the first half of the x range, ceiling up to 2x observed max mrm_prior(midpoint_range = c(0.05, 0.5), ceiling_max = 2) #> mrm_prior specification: #>   midpoint range : [0.05, 0.5] (x-axis fraction) #>   ceiling max    : 2 x observed max of y #>   floor min      : 0 (original data units)  # Allow a non-zero floor mrm_prior(floor_min = 100) #> mrm_prior specification: #>   midpoint range : [0.1, 0.9] (x-axis fraction) #>   ceiling max    : 5 x observed max of y #>   floor min      : 100 (original data units)

title: Generate a channel-level summary from a fitted response model — mrm_summary
text: standard (non-log) curves, three summary points : range_min: Spend peak marginal return (MR).     channel -invested. range_peak: Spend peak absolute return (AR).     efficient operating point. range_max: Spend MR declined     mr_decay × peak MR. Beyond , diminishing returns accelerate. log-form curves (log_logistic, weibull, reflected_weibull) monotonically decreasing MR (.e. interior efficiency peak), three summary points anchored MR fractions around current spend: range_min: Last spend level MR >= 2x MR current spend. range_peak: Current spend (operational anchor). range_max: First spend level current MR <= 0.5x MR current spend.

what: Optimize media mix allocation across channels — opt_mix
title: Optimize media mix allocation across channels — opt_mix
text: Given set fitted response curve models, find optimal spend allocation. Supports point-estimate optimization (fast, single solution) posterior-sampling optimization (slower, returns distribution solutions reflecting Bayesian uncertainty).

title: Optimize media mix allocation across channels — opt_mix
code: opt_mix(   mrms,   method = c("point", "posterior"),   objective = "max_kpi",   budget = NULL,   n_weeks = 1,   constraints = NULL,   bounds_multiplier = 3,   n_draws = 200,   seed = NULL,   parallel = FALSE,   xtol_rel = 1e-08,   maxeval = 1000,   verbose = TRUE )

title: Optimize media mix allocation across channels — opt_mix
text: mrms named list `mrmfit` objects (one per channel). method One `"point"` (default) `"posterior"`. Point uses posterior median parameters; posterior optimizes multiple MCMC draws. objective One `"max_kpi"` (default): maximize total KPI given budget constraint. budget Total budget period. `NULL` (default), inferred total current weekly spend across channels. n_weeks Number weeks budget covers. Used convert period budget weekly optimization. Default `1` (budget already weekly). Convenience values: use `52` annual, `13` quarterly, `4` monthly. constraints data frame per-channel bounds. Must contain columns `channel`, `min_spend`, `max_spend`. `NULL` (default), constraints auto-generated model return rate ranges. bounds_multiplier `constraints` `NULL`, multiplier applied auto-detected spend ranges. Default `3`. n_draws Number posterior draws optimize `method = "posterior"`. Default `200`. seed Random seed draw sampling. Default `NULL`. parallel Logical; use `future.apply` parallel posterior optimization. Default `FALSE`. Requires `future::plan()` set. xtol_rel Relative tolerance nloptr. Default `1e-8`. maxeval Maximum nloptr evaluations per solve. Default `1000`. verbose Print progress information. Default `TRUE`.

title: Optimize media mix allocation across channels — opt_mix
text: `method = "point"`: list components `solution` (tibble   optimal weekly period allocations), `constraints` (tibble),   `budget_info` (list), `nloptr_result` (raw solver output). `method = "posterior"`: list components `solution_summary`   (tibble median/CI channel), `draws_matrix` (matrix   solutions), `solution_draws` (tibble long form), `budget_info`.
